pattern_recognition: Dynamic Programming - This problem can be recognized as a dynamic programming problem because it involves breaking down the problem into smaller subproblems. The signals include the need to check multiple segments of the string and the overlapping subproblems that can be solved using previously computed results.

approaches.title: Brute Force
approaches.intuition: Check all possible ways to segment the string and see if any combination matches the dictionary.
approaches.algorithm: Recursively try to segment the string by checking each prefix against the dictionary and then recursively checking the remaining substring.

approaches.title: Optimal Approach
approaches.intuition: Use dynamic programming to store results of subproblems to avoid redundant calculations.
approaches.algorithm: Create a boolean array where each index represents whether the substring up to that index can be segmented into dictionary words. Iterate through the string and update the array based on previous results.

dry_run: Consider the input s = 'leetcode' and dict = ['leet', 'code']. Start by checking if 'leet' is in the dictionary. It is, so check the remaining substring 'code'. 'code' is also in the dictionary. Therefore, 'leetcode' can be segmented as 'leet code'.
edge_cases: Empty input should return true. A single character input that is not in the dictionary should return false. Duplicates in the dictionary should not affect the outcome. Large inputs should be handled efficiently, and overflow issues should be considered when using large strings.
common_pitfalls: 1) Assuming that the order of words in the dictionary matters; it does not. 2) Forgetting to check all prefixes of the string; only checking the first few may lead to incorrect results. 3) Not using memoization or dynamic programming, leading to excessive recursion and time complexity.
